subject: Physics
question_text: Each side of a cube is measured to be 7.203 m. What are the total surface area and the volume of the cube to appropriate significant figures?
question_html: Each side of a cube is measured to be 7.203 m. What are the total surface area and the volume of the cube to appropriate significant figures?
solution_text: Here it is given that, Side of the cube = 7.203 m Therefore, Total Surface area = {tex}6 \times ( \text { side } ) ^ { 2 } = 6 \times ( 7.203 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} = 311.299254 m 2 = 311.3 m 2 [where the value has been rounded off to 4 significant figures] Now, Volume of the cube = (side) 3 = (7.203) 3 = 373.714754 m 3 = 373.7 m 3 [Rounded off to 4 significant figures]
solution_html:
Here it is given that, Side of the cube = 7.203 m
Therefore, Total Surface area = {tex}6 \times ( \text { side } ) ^ { 2 } = 6 \times ( 7.203 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
= 311.299254 m2
 = 311.3 m2       [where the value has been rounded off to 4 significant figures]
Now, Volume of the cube = (side)3 =  (7.203)3
 = 373.714754 m3
= 373.7 m3 [Rounded off to 4 significant figures]

subject: Physics
question_text: 5.74 g of a substance occupies 1.2 cm 3 . Express its density by keeping the significant figures in view.
question_html: 5.74 g of a substance occupies 1.2 cm3. Express its density by keeping the significant figures in view.
solution_text: Density = {tex}\frac { \text { Mass } } { \text { Volume } } = \frac { 5.74 \mathrm { g } } { 1.2 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 3 } }{/tex} = 4.783 g cm -3 = 4.8 g cm -3 [according to the significant figure rule, the final result is rounded off upto 2 significant figures(minimum number of significant figures)]
solution_html:
Density = {tex}\frac { \text { Mass } } { \text { Volume } } = \frac { 5.74 \mathrm { g } } { 1.2 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 3 } }{/tex}= 4.783 g cm-3
= 4.8 g cm-3
[according to the significant figure rule, the final result is rounded off upto 2 significant figures(minimum number of significant figures)]

subject: Physics
question_text: Let us consider an equation {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = mgh where m is the mass of the body, v its velocity, g is the acceleration due to gravity, and h is the height. Check whether this equation is dimensionally correct.
question_html: Let us consider an equation {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 = mgh where m is the mass of the body, v its velocity, g is the acceleration due to gravity, and h is the height. Check whether this equation is dimensionally correct.
solution_text: The dimensions of LHS are [M] [L T –1 ] 2 = [M] [L 2 T –2 ] = [M L 2 T –2 ] The dimensions of RHS are [M][L T –2 ] [L] = [M][L 2 T –2 ] = [M L 2 T –2 ] The dimensions of LHS and RHS are the same and hence the equation is dimensionally correct.
solution_html:
The dimensions of LHS are
[M] [L T–1]2 = [M] [L2 T–2]
= [M L2 T–2]
The dimensions of RHS are [M][L T–2] [L] = [M][L2 T–2]
= [M L2 T–2]
The dimensions of LHS and RHS are the same and hence the equation is dimensionally correct.

subject: Physics
question_text: The SI unit of energy is J = kg m 2 s –2 ; that of speed v is m s –1 and of acceleration a is m s –2 . Which of the formulae for kinetic energy (K) given below can you rule out on the basis of dimensional arguments (m stands for the mass of the body) : K = m 2 v 3 K = (1/2)mv 2 K = ma K = (3/16)mv 2 K = (1/2)mv 2 + ma
question_html:
The SI unit of energy is J = kg m2 s–2; that of speed v is m s–1 and of acceleration a is m s–2. Which of the formulae for kinetic energy (K) given below can you rule out on the basis of dimensional arguments (m stands for the mass of the body) :

  1. K = m2 v3
  2. K = (1/2)mv2
  3. K = ma
  4. K = (3/16)mv2
  5. K = (1/2)mv2 + ma
solution_text: Every correct formula or equation must have the same dimensions on both sides of the equation. Also, only quantities with the same physical dimensions can be added or subtracted. The dimensions of the quantity on the right side are [M 2 L 3 T –3 ] for (a); [M L 2 T –2 ] for (b) and (d); [M L T –2 ] for (c). The quantity on the right side of (e) has no proper dimensions since two quantities of different dimensions have been added. Since the kinetic energy K has the dimensions of [M L 2 T –2 ], formulas (a), (c) and (e) are ruled out.The correct formula for kinetic energy is K = (1/2)mv 2
solution_html: Every correct formula or equation must have the same dimensions on both sides of the equation. Also, only quantities with the same physical dimensions can be added or subtracted. The dimensions of the quantity on the right side are [M2 L3 T–3] for (a); [M L2 T–2] for (b) and (d); [M L T–2] for (c). The quantity on the right side of (e) has no proper dimensions since two quantities of different dimensions have been added. Since the kinetic energy K has the dimensions of [M L2 T–2], formulas (a), (c) and (e) are ruled out.The correct formula for kinetic energy is K = (1/2)mv2

subject: Physics
question_text: Consider a simple pendulum, having a bob attached to a string, that oscillates under the action of the force of gravity. Suppose that the period of oscillation of the simple pendulum depends on its length (l), mass of the bob (m) and acceleration due to gravity (g). Derive the expression for its time period using method of dimensions.
question_html: Consider a simple pendulum, having a bob attached to a string, that oscillates under the action of the force of gravity. Suppose that the period of oscillation of the simple pendulum depends on its length (l), mass of the bob (m) and acceleration due to gravity (g). Derive the expression for its time period using method of dimensions.
solution_text: Let us assume that T {tex}\propto m ^ { a } l ^ { b } g ^ { c }{/tex} or, T = km a l b g c ...(i) where, k is a dimensionless constant. The dimensions of various quantities are [T] = T, [m] = M, [l] = L, and [g] = LT -2 Substitute these values in Eq.(i), we obtain T = [M] a [L] b [LT -2 ] c or, M 0 L 0 T 1 = M a L b+c T -2c Now equate the powers of M, L and T on both sides, we obtain a = 0, b + c = 0, -2c = 1 On solving, a = 0, {tex}b = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \quad c = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore T = k m ^ { 0 } l ^ { 1 / 2 } \mathrm { g } ^ { - 1 / 2 } = k \sqrt { \frac { l } { g } }{/tex} From experiments, k = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} Therefore, T = {tex} 2 \pi\sqrt { \frac { l } { g } }{/tex} , which is the required expression.
solution_html:
Let us assume that T {tex}\propto m ^ { a } l ^ { b } g ^ { c }{/tex}
or, T = kmalbgc  ...(i)
where, k is a dimensionless constant.
The dimensions of various quantities are
   [T] = T, [m] = M,
   [l] = L, and [g] = LT-2
Substitute these values in Eq.(i), we obtain
  T = [M]a[L]b[LT-2]c
or, M0L0T1 = MaLb+cT-2c
Now equate the powers of M, L and T on both sides, we obtain
   a = 0, b + c = 0, -2c = 1
On solving, a = 0, {tex}b = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \quad c = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore T = k m ^ { 0 } l ^ { 1 / 2 } \mathrm { g } ^ { - 1 / 2 } = k \sqrt { \frac { l } { g } }{/tex}
From experiments, k = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}
Therefore, T = {tex} 2 \pi\sqrt { \frac { l } { g } }{/tex}, which is the required expression.

subject: Physics
question_text: A calorie is a unit of heat (energy in transit) and it equals about 4.2 J, where 1 J = 1 kg m 2 s 2 . Suppose we employ a system of units in which the unit of mass equals {tex}\alpha{/tex} kg, the unit of length is {tex}\beta{/tex} m, the unit of time is {tex}\gamma{/tex} s. Show that a calorie has a magnitude 4.2 {tex}\alpha ^ { - 1 } \beta ^ { - 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 }{/tex} in terms of new units.
question_html: A calorie is a unit of heat (energy in transit) and it equals about 4.2 J, where 1 J = 1 kg m2 s2. Suppose we employ a system of units in which the unit of mass equals {tex}\alpha{/tex} kg, the unit of length is {tex}\beta{/tex} m, the unit of time is {tex}\gamma{/tex} s. Show that a calorie has a magnitude 4.2 {tex}\alpha ^ { - 1 } \beta ^ { - 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 }{/tex} in terms of new units.
solution_text: The dimensional formula of energy or heat = u = [ ML 2 T -2 ]. Assume that, M 1 , L 1 , T 1 and M 2 , L 2 , T 2 are the units of mass, length and time in given two systems. {tex}\therefore{/tex} M 1 = 1 kg, M 2 = {tex}\alpha{/tex} kg L 1 = 1 m, L 2 = {tex}\beta{/tex} m T 1 = 1 s, T 2 = {tex}\gamma{/tex} s n 1 and n 2 are the magnitudes of heat or energy of system one and system two respectively. For a given physical quantity, the product of its magnitude and unit is always constant. n 1 u 1 = n 2 u 2 or {tex}n _ { 2 } = n _ { 1 } \frac { u _ { 1 } } { u _ { 2 } } = 4.2 \times \frac { \left[ \mathrm { M } _ { 1 } \mathrm { L } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { T } _ { 1 } ^ { - 2 } \right] } { \left[ \mathrm { M } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { T } _ { 2 } ^ { - 2 } \right] }{/tex} {tex}= 4.2 \left[ \frac { \mathrm { M } _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { M } _ { 2 } } \right] \times \left[ \frac { \mathrm { L } _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { L } _ { 2 } } \right] ^ { 2 } \times \left[ \frac { \mathrm { T } _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { T } _ { 2 } } \right] ^ { - 2 }{/tex} {tex}= 4.2 \left[ \frac { 1 } { \alpha } \right] \times \left[ \frac { 1 } { \beta } \right] ^ { 2 } \times \left[ \frac { 1 } { \gamma } \right] ^ { - 2 }{/tex} n 2 = 4.2 {tex}\alpha ^ { - 1 } \beta ^ { - 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 }{/tex} new unit Therefore, 1 cal = {tex}4.2 \alpha ^ { - 1 } \beta ^ { - 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 }{/tex} new unit
solution_html:
The dimensional formula of energy or heat = u = [ ML2T-2 ].
Assume that, M1, L1, T1 and M2, L2, T2 are the units of mass, length and time in given two systems.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} M1 = 1 kg, M2 = {tex}\alpha{/tex} kg
L1 = 1 m, L2 = {tex}\beta{/tex} m
T1 = 1 s, T2 = {tex}\gamma{/tex} s
n1 and n2 are the magnitudes of heat or energy of system one and system two respectively.
For a given physical quantity, the product of its magnitude and unit is always constant.
n1u1 = n2u2
or {tex}n _ { 2 } = n _ { 1 } \frac { u _ { 1 } } { u _ { 2 } } = 4.2 \times \frac { \left[ \mathrm { M } _ { 1 } \mathrm { L } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { T } _ { 1 } ^ { - 2 } \right] } { \left[ \mathrm { M } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { T } _ { 2 } ^ { - 2 } \right] }{/tex}
{tex}= 4.2 \left[ \frac { \mathrm { M } _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { M } _ { 2 } } \right] \times \left[ \frac { \mathrm { L } _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { L } _ { 2 } } \right] ^ { 2 } \times \left[ \frac { \mathrm { T } _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { T } _ { 2 } } \right] ^ { - 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= 4.2 \left[ \frac { 1 } { \alpha } \right] \times \left[ \frac { 1 } { \beta } \right] ^ { 2 } \times \left[ \frac { 1 } { \gamma } \right] ^ { - 2 }{/tex}
n2 = 4.2{tex}\alpha ^ { - 1 } \beta ^ { - 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 }{/tex} new unit
Therefore, 1 cal = {tex}4.2 \alpha ^ { - 1 } \beta ^ { - 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 }{/tex} new unit

subject: Physics
question_text: Explain this statement clearly: To call a dimensional quantity large or small is meaningless without specifying a standard for comparison. In view of this, reframe the following statements wherever necessary: atoms are very small objects. a jet plane moves with great speed. the mass of Jupiter is very large. the air inside this room contains a large number of molecules. a proton is much more massive than an electron. the speed of sound is much smaller than the speed of light.
question_html:
Explain this statement clearly:
To call a dimensional quantity large or small is meaningless without specifying a standard for comparison. In view of this, reframe the following statements wherever necessary:

  1. atoms are very small objects.
  2. a jet plane moves with great speed.
  3. the mass of Jupiter is very large.
  4. the air inside this room contains a large number of molecules.
  5. a proton is much more massive than an electron.
  6. the speed of sound is much smaller than the speed of light.
solution_text: Measurement is basically a comparison process. Without specifying a standard of comparison, it is not possible to get an exact idea about the magnitude of a dimensional quantity. For example, the mass of a human being is 40 kg, which is very small in comparison to the mass of the earth (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 kg) but very large in comparison to the mass of an electron (9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg). An atom is a very small object when we compare it with a cricket ball. A jet plane moves with a speed greater when we compare it with a motor bike. The mass of Jupiter is very large as compared to the mass of Venus. The air inside this room contains a large number of molecules than in one mole of air. The given statement is already correct. Speed of sound is less than the speed of light. Speed of sound 346 m/ sec while speed of light is 3 x 10 8 m / sec in air at 25 0 C, hence statement is true.
solution_html:
Measurement is basically a comparison process. Without specifying a standard of comparison, it is not possible to get an exact idea about the magnitude of a dimensional quantity. For example, the mass of a human being is 40 kg, which is very small in comparison to the mass of the earth (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 kg) but very large in comparison to the mass of an electron (9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg).

  1.  An atom is a very small object when we compare it with a cricket ball.
  2. A jet plane moves with a speed greater when we compare it with a motor bike.
  3. The mass of Jupiter is very large as compared to the mass of Venus.
  4. The air inside this room contains a large number of molecules than in one mole of air.
  5. The given statement is already correct.
  6. Speed of sound is less than the speed of light. Speed of sound 346 m/ sec while speed of light is 3 x 108 m / sec in air at 25 0C, hence statement is true.

subject: Physics
question_text: A new unit of length is chosen such that the speed of light in a vacuum is unity. What is the distance between the sun and the earth in terms of the new unit, if light takes 8 min and 20 s to cover this distance?
question_html: A new unit of length is chosen such that the speed of light in a vacuum is unity. What is the distance between the sun and the earth in terms of the new unit, if light takes 8 min and 20 s  to cover this distance?
solution_text: The speed of light in vacuum (c) = 1 new unit of length s -1 Time taken by light to reach the earth, t = 8 min + 20 s = (8 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 + 20) s = 500 s Distance between the sun and the earth = Speed of light {tex}\times{/tex} Time x = c {tex}\times{/tex} t = 1 new unit of length s -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 s = 500 new unit of length.
solution_html:
The speed of light in vacuum (c) = 1 new unit of length s-1
Time taken by light to reach the earth, t = 8 min + 20 s = (8 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 + 20) s = 500 s
Distance between the sun and the earth = Speed of light {tex}\times{/tex} Time
x = c {tex}\times{/tex} t
= 1 new unit of length s-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 s
= 500 new unit of length.

subject: Physics
question_text: Which of the following is the most precise device for measuring length: a vernier callipers with 20 divisions on the sliding scale a screw gauge of pitch 1 mm and 100 divisions on the circular scale an optical instrument that can measure length to within a wavelength of light?
question_html:
Which of the following is the most precise device for measuring length:

  1. a vernier callipers with 20 divisions on the sliding scale
  2. a screw gauge of pitch 1 mm and 100 divisions on the circular scale
  3. an optical instrument that can measure length to within a wavelength of light?
solution_text: The most precise device is that whose least count is minimum. Least count of vernier calliper = 1 standard division (SD) - 1 vernier division (VD) {tex}1 S D - 19 / 20 S D = 1 / 20 m m = 0.005 c m{/tex} Least count of screw gauge = {tex}\frac { \text { Pitch } } { \text { No of divisions } }{/tex} {tex}=\frac { 1 } { 100 }mm= \frac { 1 } { 1000 } cm= 0.001 \mathrm { cm }{/tex} Least count of an optical device = Wavelength of light {tex}( \lambda ) \sim 10 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { cm }{/tex} = 0.00001 cm Hence, it can be inferred that an optical instrument is the most suitable device to measure length.
solution_html:
The most precise device is that whose least count is minimum.

  1. Least count of vernier calliper
    = 1 standard division (SD) - 1 vernier division (VD)
    {tex}1 S D - 19 / 20 S D = 1 / 20 m m = 0.005 c m{/tex}
  2. Least count of screw gauge = {tex}\frac { \text { Pitch } } { \text { No of divisions } }{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac { 1 } { 100 }mm= \frac { 1 } { 1000 } cm= 0.001 \mathrm { cm }{/tex}
  3. Least count of an optical device = Wavelength of light {tex}( \lambda ) \sim 10 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { cm }{/tex}
    = 0.00001 cm
    Hence, it can be inferred that an optical instrument is the most suitable device to measure length.

subject: Physics
question_text: A student measures the thickness of a human hair by looking at it through a microscope of magnification 100. He makes 20 observations and finds that the average width of the hair in the field of view of the microscope is 3.5 mm. What is the estimate on the thickness of hair?
question_html: A student measures the thickness of a human hair by looking at it through a microscope of magnification 100. He makes 20 observations and finds that the average width of the hair in the field of view of the microscope is 3.5 mm. What is the estimate on the thickness of hair?
solution_text: Magnification of the microscope = 100 Average width of the hair in the field of view of the microscope = 3.5 mm {tex}\therefore{/tex} Actual thickness of the hair is {tex}=\frac { 3.5 } { 100 }{/tex} = 0.035 mm.
solution_html:
Magnification of the microscope = 100
Average width of the hair in the field of view of the microscope = 3.5 mm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Actual thickness of the hair is {tex}=\frac { 3.5 } { 100 }{/tex} = 0.035 mm.

subject: Physics
question_text: Answer the following: You are given a thread and a metre scale. How will you estimate the diameter of the thread? A screw gauge has a pitch of 1.0 mm and 200 divisions on the circular scale. Do you think it is possible to increase the accuracy of the screw gauge arbitrarily by increasing the number of divisions on the circular scale? The mean diameter of a thin brass rod is to be measured by vernier calliper. Why is a set of 100 measurements of the diameter expected to yield a more reliable estimate than a set of 5 measurements only?
question_html:
Answer the following:

  1. You are given a thread and a metre scale. How will you estimate the diameter of the thread?
  2. A screw gauge has a pitch of 1.0 mm and 200 divisions on the circular scale. Do you think it is possible to increase the accuracy of the screw gauge arbitrarily by increasing the number of divisions on the circular scale?
  3. The mean diameter of a thin brass rod is to be measured by vernier calliper. Why is a set of 100 measurements of the diameter expected to yield a more reliable estimate than a set of 5 measurements only?
solution_text: The diameter of a thread is so small that it cannot be measured using a metre scale. We wind a number of turns of the thread on the meter scale so that the turns are closely touching one another. Measure the length ( {tex}l{/tex} ) of the winding on the scale which contains n number of turns. The diameter of thread = {tex}\frac{l}{n}{/tex} As least count {tex}=\frac{\text { pitch }}{\text { number of divisions on circular scale }}{/tex} Therefore, theoretically speaking least count decreases on increasing the number of divisions on the circular scale. Hence, accuracy would increase. Practically it may not be possible to take the reading precisely due to low resolution of human eye. Larger the number of readings, closer is the arithmetic mean to the true value and hence smaller the random error. Hence result with a set of 100 measurements is more reliable than that with a set of 5 measurements.
solution_html:
1. The diameter of a thread is so small that it cannot be measured using a metre scale. We wind a number of turns of the thread on the meter scale so that the turns are closely touching one another.
    Measure the length ({tex}l{/tex}) of the winding on the scale which contains n number of turns. The diameter of thread = {tex}\frac{l}{n}{/tex}
  2. As least count {tex}=\frac{\text { pitch }}{\text { number of divisions on circular scale }}{/tex}
    Therefore, theoretically speaking least count decreases on increasing the number of divisions on the circular scale. Hence, accuracy would increase. Practically it may not be possible to take the reading precisely due to low resolution of human eye.
  3. Larger the number of readings, closer is the arithmetic mean to the true value and hence smaller the random error. Hence result with a set of 100 measurements is more reliable than that with a set of 5 measurements.

subject: Physics
question_text: The photograph of a house occupies an area of 1.75 cm 2 on a 35 mm slide. The slide is projected on to a screen and the area of the house on the screen is 1.55 m 2 . What is the linear magnification of the projector-screen arrangement?
question_html: The photograph of a house occupies an area of 1.75 cm2 on a 35 mm slide. The slide is projected on to a screen and the area of the house on the screen is 1.55 m2. What is the linear magnification of the projector-screen arrangement?
solution_text: Area of the house on the slide = 1.75 cm 2 Area of the image of the house formed on the screen = 1.55 m 2 = 1.55 × 10 4 cm 2 Arial Magnification, m a = {tex}\frac{\text {Area of Image}}{\text {Area of object}}=\frac{1.55}{1.75} \times 10^{4}{/tex} Linear magnifications, m l = {tex}\sqrt { m _ { a } }{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt { \frac { 1.55 } { 1.75 } \times 10 ^ { 4 } } = 94.11{/tex}
solution_html:
Area of the house on the slide = 1.75 cm2
Area of the image of the house formed on the screen = 1.55 m2 = 1.55 × 104 cm2
Arial Magnification, ma = {tex}\frac{\text {Area of Image}}{\text {Area of object}}=\frac{1.55}{1.75} \times 10^{4}{/tex}
Linear magnifications, ml ={tex}\sqrt { m _ { a } }{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt { \frac { 1.55 } { 1.75 } \times 10 ^ { 4 } } = 94.11{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: State the number of significant figures in the following : 0.007 m 2 2.64 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 kg 0.2370 g cm -3 6.320 J 6.032 N m -2 0.0006032 m 2
question_html:
State the number of significant figures in the following :

  1. 0.007 m2
  2. 2.64 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 kg
  3. 0.2370 g cm-3
  4. 6.320 J
  5. 6.032 N m-2
  6. 0.0006032 m2
solution_text: 1. If the number is less than one, then all zeros on the right of the decimal point are insignificant. This means that here, two zeros after the decimal are not significant. Hence, only 7 is a significant figure in this quantity. 3. For the determination of significant values, we do not consider the power of 10 (Number is not less than 1). The digits 2, 6, and 4 are significant figures. Hence, It has 3 significant digits. 4. Explanation: Significant figure- 2, 3, 7, 0. Trailing 0’s is significant. These 0’s increase the accuracy of the answer. 4. From the condition of a significant figure, the zero after the decimal point comes after a non-zero number so it significant figure. The number of significant figures is 4. 4. Explanation: Significant figure- 6, 0, 3, 2. 0’s between 2 non-zero digits are significant. 4. Explanation: Significant figure- 6, 0, 3, 2. Since the number is less than 1.
solution_html:
1. 1. If the number is less than one, then all zeros on the right of the decimal point are insignificant. This means that here, two zeros after the decimal are not significant. Hence, only 7 is a significant figure in this quantity.
  2. 3. For the determination of significant values, we do not consider the power of 10 (Number is not less than 1). The digits 2, 6, and 4 are significant figures. Hence, It has 3 significant digits.
  3. 4. Explanation: Significant figure- 2, 3, 7, 0. Trailing 0’s is significant. These 0’s increase the accuracy of the answer.
  4. 4. From the condition of a significant figure, the zero after the decimal point comes after a non-zero number so it significant figure. The number of significant figures is 4.
  5. 4. Explanation: Significant figure- 6, 0, 3, 2. 0’s between 2 non-zero digits are significant.
  6. 4. Explanation: Significant figure- 6, 0, 3, 2. Since the number is less than 1.

subject: Physics
question_text: The length, breadth and thickness of a rectangular sheet of metal are 4.234 m, 1.005m, and 2.01 cm respectively. Give the area and volume of the sheet to correct significant figures.
question_html: The length, breadth and thickness of a rectangular sheet of metal are 4.234 m, 1.005m, and 2.01 cm respectively. Give the area and volume of the sheet to correct significant figures.
solution_text: Length of sheet, l = 4.234 m Breadth of sheet, b = 1.005 m Thickness of sheet, h = 2.01 cm = 0.0201 m The given table lists the respective significant figures: Qunatity Number Significant Figure l 4.234 4 b 1.005 4 h 0.0201 3 Hence, area and volume both must have at least 3 significant figures. Now, surface area of the sheet = 2 (l × b + b × h + h × l) = 2(4.234 × 1.005 + 1.005 × 0.0201 + 0.0201 × 4.234) = 2(4.25517 + 0.0202005 + 0.0851034) = 2 × 4.36 = 8.72 m2 (three significant figures) Volume of the sheet = {tex}=l\times b \times h{/tex} = 4.234 × 1.005 × 0.0201 = 0.0855 m 3 (three significant figures) This number has only 3 significant figures i.e., 8, 5, and 5.
solution_html:
Length of sheet, l = 4.234 m
Breadth of sheet, b = 1.005 m
Thickness of sheet, h = 2.01 cm = 0.0201 m
The given table lists the respective significant figures:

Qunatity Number Significant Figure
l 4.234 4
b 1.005 4
h 0.0201 3

Hence, area and volume both must have at least 3 significant figures.
Now, surface area of the sheet = 2 (l × b + b × h + h × l)
= 2(4.234 × 1.005 + 1.005 × 0.0201 + 0.0201 × 4.234)
= 2(4.25517 + 0.0202005 + 0.0851034)
= 2 × 4.36
= 8.72 m2 (three significant figures)
Volume of the sheet = {tex}=l\times b \times h{/tex}
= 4.234 × 1.005 × 0.0201
= 0.0855 m3  (three significant figures)
This number has only 3 significant figures i.e., 8, 5, and 5.

subject: Physics
question_text: The mass of a box measured by a grocer's balance is 2.300 kg. Two gold pieces of masses 20.15 g and 20.17 g are added to the box. What is (a) the total mass of the box, (b) the difference in the masses of the pieces to correct significant figures?
question_html: The mass of a box measured by a grocer's balance is 2.300 kg. Two gold pieces of masses 20.15 g and 20.17 g are added to the box. What is (a) the total mass of the box, (b) the difference in the masses of the pieces to correct significant figures?
solution_text: Mass of grocer's box = 2.300 kg Mass of gold piece I = 20.15g = 0.02015 kg Mass of gold piece II = 20.17 g = 0.02017 kg Total mass of the box = 2.3 + 0.02015 + 0.02017 = 2.34032 kg In addition, the final result should retain as many decimal places as there are in the number with the least decimal places. Hence, the total mass of the box is 2.3 kg. Difference in masses = 20.17 - 20.15 = 0.02 g In subtraction, the final result should retain as many decimal places as there are in the number with the least decimal places.
solution_html:
Mass of grocer's box = 2.300 kg
Mass of gold piece I = 20.15g = 0.02015 kg
Mass of gold piece II = 20.17 g = 0.02017 kg

  1. Total mass of the box = 2.3 + 0.02015 + 0.02017 = 2.34032 kg
    In addition, the final result should retain as many decimal places as there are in the number with the least decimal places. Hence, the total mass of the box is 2.3 kg.
  2. Difference in masses = 20.17 - 20.15 = 0.02 g
    In subtraction, the final result should retain as many decimal places as there are in the number with the least decimal places.

subject: Physics
question_text: A famous relation in physics relates moving mass m to the rest mass m o of a particle in terms of its speed v and the speed of light, c. (This relation first arose as a consequence of special relativity due to Albert Einstein). A boy recalls the relation almost correctly but forgets where to put the constant c. He writes: {tex}m = \frac { m _ { 0 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } }{/tex} . Guess where to put the missing c?
question_html:
A famous relation in physics relates moving mass m to the rest mass mo of a particle in terms of its speed v and the speed of light, c. (This relation first arose as a consequence of special relativity due to Albert Einstein). A boy recalls the relation almost correctly but forgets where to put the constant c. He writes:
{tex}m = \frac { m _ { 0 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } }{/tex}. Guess where to put the missing c?
solution_text: The given formula will be dimensionally correct only when the dimension of L.H.S will be same as that of R.H.S. This is only possible when the factor, {tex}\left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }{/tex} is dimensionless i.e., {tex}\left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} is dimensionless. This can only possible when v 2 is divided by the square of another velocity term. And here that term is c 2 . So, {tex}\mathrm { v } ^ { 2 }{/tex} should be divided by {tex}c ^ { 2 }{/tex} to make the denominator of the fraction dimensionless. Hence, the correct relation is {tex}{ m } = \frac { { m } _ { 0 } } { \left( 1 - \frac { \mathrm { v } ^ { 2 } } { \mathrm { c } ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } }{/tex} From this relativistic mass formula, one can easily see that when the velocity of a particle becomes comparable to the speed of light, its mass increases.
solution_html: The given formula will be dimensionally correct only when the dimension of L.H.S will be same as that of R.H.S. This is only possible when the factor, {tex}\left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }{/tex} is dimensionless i.e., {tex}\left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}is dimensionless. This can only possible when v2 is divided by the square of another velocity term. And here that term is c2. So, {tex}\mathrm { v } ^ { 2 }{/tex} should be divided by {tex}c ^ { 2 }{/tex} to make the denominator of the fraction dimensionless. Hence, the correct relation is {tex}{ m } = \frac { { m } _ { 0 } } { \left( 1 - \frac { \mathrm { v } ^ { 2 } } { \mathrm { c } ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } }{/tex}From this relativistic mass formula, one can easily see that when the velocity of a particle becomes comparable to the speed of light, its mass increases.

subject: Physics
question_text: The unit of length convenient on the atomic scale is known as an angstrom and is denoted by {tex}\stackrel { \circ } { A }{/tex} ( {tex}1\stackrel { \circ } { A } = 10^{-10}m{/tex} ). The size of a hydrogen atom is about 0.5 {tex}\stackrel { \circ } { A }{/tex} . What is the total atomic volume in m 3 of a mole of hydrogen atoms?
question_html: The unit of length convenient on the atomic scale is known as an angstrom and is denoted by {tex}\stackrel { \circ } { A }{/tex} ( {tex}1\stackrel { \circ } { A } = 10^{-10}m{/tex}). The size of a hydrogen atom is about 0.5{tex}\stackrel { \circ } { A }{/tex}. What is the total atomic volume in m3 of a mole of hydrogen atoms?
solution_text: Radius of hydrogen atom, r = 0.5 {tex}A = 0.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } m{/tex} Volume of hydrogen atom {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times \left( 0.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= 0.524 \times 10 ^ { - 30 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex} 1 mole of hydrogen contains {tex}6.023 \times 10 ^ { 23 }{/tex} hydrogen atoms. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of 1 mole of hydrogen atoms {tex}= 6.023 \times 10 ^ { 23 } \times 0.524 \times 10 ^ { - 30 }{/tex} {tex}= 3.16 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } m ^ { 3 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Radius of hydrogen atom, r = 0.5 {tex}A = 0.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } m{/tex}
Volume of hydrogen atom {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times \left( 0.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= 0.524 \times 10 ^ { - 30 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex}
1 mole of hydrogen contains {tex}6.023 \times 10 ^ { 23 }{/tex} hydrogen atoms.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of 1 mole of hydrogen atoms {tex}= 6.023 \times 10 ^ { 23 } \times 0.524 \times 10 ^ { - 30 }{/tex}
{tex}= 3.16 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } m ^ { 3 }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: One mole of an ideal gas at standard temperature and pressure occupies 22.4 L (molar volume). What is the ratio of molar volume to the atomic volume of a mole of hydrogen? (Take the size of hydrogen molecule to be about 1 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} ). Why is this ratio so large?
question_html: One mole of an ideal gas at standard temperature and pressure occupies 22.4 L (molar volume). What is the ratio of molar volume to the atomic volume of a mole of hydrogen? (Take the size of hydrogen molecule to be about 1{tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}). Why is this ratio so large?
solution_text: Radius of one single hydrogen atom, r = 0.5 {tex}\mathop A\limits^0{/tex} = {tex}0.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 }{/tex} m (1 {tex}\mathop A\limits^0{/tex} = 10 -10 m) Volume of one single hydrogen atom {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times \left( 0.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= 0.524 \times 10 ^ { - 30 } { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex} Now, 1 mole of hydrogen atoms means {tex}6.023 \times 10 ^ { 23 }{/tex} number of hydrogen atoms.(according to Avogadro's hypothesis) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Atomic volume of 1 mole of hydrogen atoms, {tex}V _ { H _ { 2 } }{/tex} = Avogadro's number {tex}\times{/tex} volume of one single hydrogen atom {tex}= 6.023 \times 10 ^ { 23 } \times 0.524 \times 10 ^ { - 30 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= 3.16 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex} Molar volume of 1 mole of hydrogen atoms at STP, {tex} { V } _ { m } = 22.4 L = 22.4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex} (1 litre = 10 -3 m 3 ) Now, ratio of molar volume of 22.4 L of H 2 to the atomic volume of 1 mole of H 2 , {tex}\therefore \frac { V _ { m } } { V _ { H_2 } } = \frac { 22.4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 3.16 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } = 7.08 \times 10 ^ { 4 }{/tex} Hence, the molar volume is {tex}7.08 \times 10 ^ { 4 }{/tex} times higher than the atomic volume. This ratio is very high due to the fact that the inter-atomic distance is very high as compared to the size of atoms in hydrogen gas.
solution_html:
Radius of one single hydrogen atom, r = 0.5 {tex}\mathop A\limits^0{/tex} = {tex}0.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 }{/tex} m (1 {tex}\mathop A\limits^0{/tex} = 10-10 m)
Volume of one single hydrogen atom {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times \left( 0.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= 0.524 \times 10 ^ { - 30 } { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex}
Now, 1 mole of hydrogen atoms means {tex}6.023 \times 10 ^ { 23 }{/tex}  number of hydrogen atoms.(according to Avogadro's hypothesis)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Atomic volume of 1 mole of hydrogen atoms, {tex}V _ { H _ { 2 } }{/tex} = Avogadro's number {tex}\times{/tex} volume of one single hydrogen atom {tex}= 6.023 \times 10 ^ { 23 } \times 0.524 \times 10 ^ { - 30 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= 3.16 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex}
Molar volume of 1 mole of hydrogen atoms at STP,
{tex} { V } _ { m } = 22.4 L = 22.4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex} (1 litre = 10-3 m3)

Now, ratio of molar volume of 22.4 L of H2 to the atomic volume of 1 mole of H2,
{tex}\therefore \frac { V _ { m } } { V _ { H_2 } } = \frac { 22.4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 3.16 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } = 7.08 \times 10 ^ { 4 }{/tex}
Hence, the molar volume is {tex}7.08 \times 10 ^ { 4 }{/tex} times higher than the atomic volume. This ratio is very high due to the fact that the inter-atomic distance is very high as compared to the size of atoms in hydrogen gas.

subject: Physics
question_text: Explain this common observation clearly: If you look out of the window of a fast moving train, the nearby trees, houses etc. seem to move rapidly in a direction opposite to the train's motion, but the distant objects (hill tops, the Moon, the stars etc.) seem to be stationary. (In fact, since you are aware that you are moving, these distant objects seem to move with you).
question_html: Explain this common observation clearly: If you look out of the window of a fast moving train, the nearby trees, houses etc. seem to move rapidly in a direction opposite to the train's motion, but the distant objects (hill tops, the Moon, the stars etc.) seem to be stationary. (In fact, since you are aware that you are moving, these distant objects seem to move with you).
solution_text: The line joining a given object to our eye is known as the line of sight. When a train moves rapidly, the line of sight of a passenger sitting in the train for nearby trees changes its direction rapidly. As a result, the nearby trees and other objects appear to run in a direction opposite to the train's motion. However, the line of sight of distant and large size objects e.g., hilltops, the Moon, the stars etc., almost remains unchanged (or changes by an extremely small angle). As a result, the distant object seems to be stationary.
solution_html: The line joining a given object to our eye is known as the line of sight. When a train moves rapidly, the line of sight of a passenger sitting in the train for nearby trees changes its direction rapidly. As a result, the nearby trees and other objects appear to run in a direction opposite to the train's motion. However, the line of sight of distant and large size objects e.g., hilltops, the Moon, the stars etc., almost remains unchanged (or changes by an extremely small angle). As a result, the distant object seems to be stationary.

subject: Physics
question_text: The Sun is a hot plasma (ionized matter) with its inner core at a temperature exceeding 10 7 K, and its outer surface at a temperature of about 6000 K. At these high temperatures, no substance remains in a solid or liquid phase. In what range do you expect the mass density of the Sun to be, in the range of densities of solids and liquids or gases? Check if your guess is correct from the following data: mass of the Sun {tex}= 2.0 \times 10 ^ { 30 }{/tex} kg, radius of the Sun {tex}= 7.0 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
question_html: The Sun is a hot plasma (ionized matter) with its inner core at a temperature exceeding 107 K, and its outer surface at a temperature of about 6000 K. At these high temperatures, no substance remains in a solid or liquid phase. In what range do you expect the mass density of the Sun to be, in the range of densities of solids and liquids or gases? Check if your guess is correct from the following data: mass of the Sun {tex}= 2.0 \times 10 ^ { 30 }{/tex} kg, radius of the Sun {tex}= 7.0 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
solution_text: Mass of the Sun, {tex}M = 2.0 \times 10 ^ { 30 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex} Radius of the Sun, {tex}R = 7.0 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Volume of the Sun, {tex}V= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times \left( 7.0 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 88 } { 21 } \times 343 \times 10 ^ { 24 } = 1437.3 \times 10 ^ { 24 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex} Density of the Sun = {tex}\frac { \text { Mass } } { \text { Volume } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2.0 \times 10 ^ { 30 } } { 1437.3 \times 10 ^ { 24 } } \approx 1.4 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { kg } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 3}{/tex} Hence, the density of the Sun here is in the density range of solids and liquids. This high density is attributed to the intense gravitational attraction of the inner layers on the outer layer of the Sun.
solution_html:
Mass of the Sun, {tex}M = 2.0 \times 10 ^ { 30 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex}
Radius of the Sun, {tex}R = 7.0 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Volume of the Sun, {tex}V= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times \left( 7.0 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 88 } { 21 } \times 343 \times 10 ^ { 24 } = 1437.3 \times 10 ^ { 24 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 3 }{/tex}
Density of the Sun ={tex}\frac { \text { Mass } } { \text { Volume } }{/tex}{tex}= \frac { 2.0 \times 10 ^ { 30 } } { 1437.3 \times 10 ^ { 24 } } \approx 1.4 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { kg } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 3}{/tex}

Hence, the density of the Sun here is in the density range of solids and liquids. This high density is attributed to the intense gravitational attraction of the inner layers on the outer layer of the Sun.
